title: Launcher, Editor & Your First Movement Toy
html:
Getting Unreal Engine 5 onto your machine

Unreal Engine is Epic Games' free, source-available engine that ships full 2D and 3D capability in one package. We will use it to build everything from Pong to a real first-person shooter and a Chaos-physics racing game. The first step is the Epic Games Launcher: download it from epicgames.com, sign in (or create an account), open the Unreal Engine tab, and under Library click the plus icon to install an engine version. Pick the latest UE 5.x stable release. The install is large (tens of gigabytes); choose a fast SSD and, in the install options, you can deselect platforms you do not need (Android, iOS) to save space.

Tip.
Keep the launcher around even after install — it manages engine versions, the Marketplace (free assets every month), and your project list. You can have multiple engine versions side by side.

Creating your first project

Launch the engine from the library. The Project Browser appears. Projects are grouped by category (Games, Film, Architecture). Choose Games, then the Blank template. On the right, set:

  • Blueprint vs C++ — pick Blueprint for now; we add C++ later in this course.
  • Quality Preset — Maximum on a desktop GPU, Scalable on a laptop.
  • Starter Content — enable it; it gives you free props and materials to play with.
  • Project name and folder — e.g. MovementToy. Avoid spaces and start with a letter.

Click Create. UE builds shaders the first time (this can take a few minutes), then drops you into the editor.

Touring the editor panels

The Unreal Editor is one window made of dockable panels. Learn these five first:

PanelWhat it does
ViewportThe 3D scene. Navigate, select, and place Actors here.
OutlinerA tree list of every Actor in the level.
DetailsProperties of the selected Actor (transform, components, settings).
Content BrowserYour project's assets — meshes, materials, Blueprints.
ToolbarSave, Play (PIE), build, and settings.

Camera navigation in the Viewport is muscle memory you will use constantly. Hold the right mouse button and use W A S D to fly like a first-person camera; the mouse looks around. Hold right-mouse and scroll the wheel to change fly speed. The F key frames the selected Actor. The transform gizmo modes are W (move), E (rotate), R (scale).

Placing and moving Actors

Everything you put in a level is an Actor. Open the Quickly add to the project panel (the green + in the toolbar, or drag from the Place Actors menu). Drag a Cube into the Viewport. It lands in the level and appears in the Outliner. Select it and look at the Details panel — you can type exact values into Location, Rotation, and Scale, or drag the gizmo arrows in the Viewport.

Making it actually move with a tiny Blueprint

Static placement is not a game. To make our cube a controllable movement toy, we turn it into a Blueprint Actor and feed it keyboard input. Right-click the cube in the Outliner and choose Convert selected actor to Blueprint Class, name it BP_MoverToy, and save it into a Blueprints folder. Double-click it to open the Blueprint editor, then open the Event Graph. We will read arrow keys directly (a quick approach; the proper Enhanced Input system comes in lesson 3.4) and add movement each frame.

BP_MoverToy · Event Graph (node logic, written as pseudo-graph)
// Variables to create in the My Blueprint panel: // Speed (Float) = 600.0 Event Tick (DeltaSeconds) --> AddActorWorldOffset Delta = MoveInput * Speed * DeltaSeconds Sweep = true // Build MoveInput each tick from key state: InputAxis Right Arrow (pressed) --> X += 1 InputAxis Left Arrow (pressed) --> X -= 1 InputAxis Up Arrow (pressed) --> Y += 1 InputAxis Down Arrow (pressed) --> Y -= 1 MoveInput = Normalize(Vector(X, Y, 0))

For the simplest possible version that runs today, you can instead drop four Key event nodes (Right-click → search 'Right') and have each Pressed/Released path set a vector, then add that vector on Tick. Compile, save, drag BP_MoverToy into the level, press Play in the toolbar, and steer your cube around. You just made your first interactive thing in Unreal.

Lab.
Add a Float variable Speed and expose it (the eye icon) so you can tweak it per-instance in the Details panel. Place three copies of the toy with different speeds and race them.
🎮 Build This: Setup / Foundations — Movement Toy

This is a foundations build — it starts the course, not extending any prior game. The goal is just a controllable cube proving your editor and input loop work end to end.

  1. Create a Blank Blueprint game project named MovementToy with Starter Content.
  2. Drag a floor and a Cube into the level; frame them with F.
  3. Convert the cube to BP_MoverToy and open its Event Graph.
  4. Add a Speed float (600) and an AddActorWorldOffset on Event Tick.
  5. Wire arrow-key state into a normalized MoveInput vector and feed it to the offset.
  6. Press Play and drive the cube; tune Speed in the Details panel.

title: Actors, Components & the Gameplay Framework
html:
Actors are the nouns of your world

An Actor is any object you can place in or spawn into a level — a wall, a light, a camera, a character, a trigger volume. The base class is AActor. By itself an Actor has a transform (location, rotation, scale) and a lifecycle, but it is mostly an empty shell. Its real abilities come from Components attached to it. Think of the Actor as a backpack and Components as the gear you stuff inside.

Components are the capabilities

A Component is a reusable piece of functionality owned by an Actor. There are three broad families:

Component typeExamplesHas a transform?
SceneSceneComponent, billboards, audioYes (position in space)
PrimitiveStaticMesh, SkeletalMesh, collision shapesYes + renders/collides
Actor (non-scene)MovementComponent, RotatingMovementNo (pure logic)

Every Actor with a visual or physical presence has a RootComponent — the scene component that defines where the Actor lives. Other components attach beneath it to form a hierarchy. When you move the root, the whole tree moves with it. In the Blueprint editor's Components panel you literally drag components into a parent/child tree.

Tip.
Favor composition over inheritance. Instead of writing a giant special-case Actor, build small components (health, inventory, interaction) and bolt them onto whatever Actor needs them. This is how shipping Unreal games stay maintainable.

The Gameplay Framework: who runs the show

Placing Actors is not enough — something must decide the rules, who the player is, and what camera they see through. Unreal's Gameplay Framework is a set of special Actors that wire together automatically when a level starts. Learn these five:

  • GameMode (AGameModeBase) — the rulebook. It exists only on the server/authority and defines which classes to use for the pawn, controller, HUD, etc. One per level.
  • Pawn (APawn) — a possessable Actor representing a player or AI body in the world. ACharacter is a Pawn subclass with a capsule and walking movement built in.
  • PlayerController (APlayerController) — the disembodied will behind a Pawn. It receives input and possesses a Pawn to drive it. One per human player.
  • GameState / PlayerState — replicated containers for shared match data (score, time) and per-player data (name, kills).
  • HUD — legacy on-screen drawing; modern projects use UMG widgets instead (covered later).

How they connect

When play begins, the GameMode reads its class defaults and performs the wiring: it spawns a PlayerController for each player, spawns the configured Pawn (often at a PlayerStart Actor), and tells the controller to Possess that Pawn. Input now flows Controller → Pawn, and the Pawn's camera renders to the player's screen.

framework wiring (conceptual flow)
// At level start the GameMode does, roughly: GameMode.DefaultPawnClass = BP_PlayerPawn GameMode.PlayerControllerClass = BP_PlayerController GameMode.GameStateClass = BP_GameState // then, per player: controller = SpawnPlayerController() pawn = SpawnDefaultPawnAtTransform(playerStart) controller.Possess(pawn) // input + camera now drive the pawn

Setting your own GameMode

You override the framework by creating your own classes and telling the project to use them. Create a Blueprint subclass of Game Mode Base (e.g. BP_ToyGameMode) and a Pawn subclass (BP_ToyPawn). In BP_ToyGameMode's Details, set Default Pawn Class to BP_ToyPawn. Then in Project Settings → Maps & Modes, set the Default GameMode to BP_ToyGameMode. You can also override per-level via World Settings → GameMode Override, which is handy when different levels follow different rules (a menu vs a match).

Lab.
Open the Pawn from lesson 3.1's toy. In its Components panel, add a Static Mesh (set to a cube), a child Spring Arm, and a Camera on the arm. Notice how moving the root mesh drags the camera tree along — that is the component hierarchy at work.
Watch out.
Only one GameMode runs per level. If your input or camera silently does nothing, check that the active GameMode's Default Pawn Class is the Pawn you expect — the framework will happily spawn the wrong (or a default) Pawn and possess that instead.
🎮 Build This: Engine Architecture — Framework Sandbox

This is an architecture build that extends the Movement Toy from lesson 3.1, wrapping it in a proper Gameplay Framework instead of a loose Actor.

  1. Create BP_ToyPawn (Pawn subclass) with a StaticMesh root, a SpringArm, and a Camera.
  2. Give the pawn a Floating Pawn Movement component and feed input into AddMovementInput.
  3. Create BP_ToyGameMode and set its Default Pawn Class to BP_ToyPawn.
  4. In Project Settings, set the project's Default GameMode to BP_ToyGameMode.
  5. Place a PlayerStart in the level and press Play — the framework spawns and possesses your pawn automatically.
  6. Confirm the camera follows because it is a child component of the possessed pawn.

title: Blueprints: Visual Scripting for Gameplay
html:
What Blueprints are

Blueprints are Unreal's visual scripting system: you author gameplay by wiring nodes together with wires instead of typing code. A Blueprint is a real class — it can subclass any C++ class (Actor, Pawn, GameMode) and add variables and functions. Under the hood Blueprints compile to bytecode run by a virtual machine. They are fast to iterate on, designer-friendly, and the right tool for most gameplay logic; C++ (lesson 3.6) is for performance-critical systems and engine extension. A mature project mixes both.

Anatomy of a Blueprint

Open any Blueprint class to find these tabs: the Components panel (the hierarchy from lesson 3.2), the My Blueprint panel (variables, functions, macros, event dispatchers), the Event Graph (where logic lives), and the Construction Script (runs in the editor when you edit/place the Actor, before play).

Execution wires vs data wires

There are two kinds of pins. Execution pins are the white arrows that define order — one event fires, then the next node, then the next. Data pins are colored by type (red bool, green float, blue object, etc.) and carry values into node inputs. You do not put data on the white wire; you feed it into the side pins. Reading this distinction is the single most important Blueprint skill.

The lifecycle: BeginPlay, Tick, EndPlay

Every Actor follows a lifecycle. The events you will use constantly:

EventWhen it firesTypical use
Event BeginPlayOnce, when play starts or the Actor spawnsInitialize state, cache references
Event TickEvery frame, with DeltaSecondsPer-frame movement, timers, checks
Event EndPlayWhen the Actor is destroyed or play endsCleanup, save state
Watch out.
Tick is expensive at scale. Multiply your math by DeltaSeconds so behavior is frame-rate independent, and disable Tick (Class Defaults → Start with Tick Enabled = off, or Set Actor Tick Enabled) on Actors that do not need per-frame work. Prefer Timers and events when you can.

Variables, functions, and flow control

Create variables in the My Blueprint panel; pick a type, set a default, and click the eye to make them Instance Editable (tweakable per placed Actor). Functions are reusable graphs with inputs and outputs and one entry point — use them to keep the Event Graph readable. Flow control nodes shape execution: Branch (if/else on a bool), Sequence (do A then B then C), ForLoop / ForEachLoop (iterate), Gate and DoOnce (guard re-entry), and Switch on Int/Enum.

BP_Spinner · Event Graph (node logic as pseudo-graph)
// Variables: RotateSpeed (Float) = 90.0 // degrees/sec // IsSpinning (Bool) = true Event BeginPlay --> Print String("Spinner ready") Event Tick(DeltaSeconds) --> Branch(Condition = IsSpinning) True --> AddActorLocalRotation DeltaRotation = MakeRotator(0, 0, RotateSpeed * DeltaSeconds) False --> (do nothing) // Toggle with a key: Key Space (Pressed) --> Set IsSpinning = NOT(IsSpinning)

Casting and references

To talk to another Actor you need a reference to it, then often a Cast to its specific class so you can reach its variables and functions. Get references via Get Player Pawn, Get All Actors of Class, overlap event outputs, or by exposing an object variable. After a successful Cast To BP_Door, the cast's blue output pin lets you call OpenDoor on it.

Tip.
Print String is your console.log. Drop it anywhere to confirm an event fired and inspect values during play. Set its duration and color to make on-screen debugging readable, and remember to remove or guard them before shipping.

Macros, collapsed graphs, and Event Dispatchers

As graphs grow, tidy them: select nodes and Collapse to Function/Macro. Event Dispatchers let one Blueprint broadcast an event that others subscribe to — the foundation of decoupled communication (a pickup tells the HUD it was collected without knowing the HUD exists). We will lean on dispatchers heavily once we build real games.

🎮 Build This: Scripting — Interactive Spinner & Mover

This is a scripting build that extends the Framework Sandbox from lesson 3.2 by adding scripted, lifecycle-driven Actors around your pawn.

  1. Create BP_Spinner with a StaticMesh and the rotation logic above; expose RotateSpeed as Instance Editable.
  2. On BeginPlay, Print a ready message; on Tick, branch on IsSpinning and rotate by speed times DeltaSeconds.
  3. Add a Space key event that toggles IsSpinning with a NOT node.
  4. Make a reusable Function BobUpDown that offsets Z by a sine of Game Time Seconds; call it from Tick.
  5. Place several spinners with different RotateSpeed values and drive your pawn between them.
  6. Add an Event Dispatcher that fires when the pawn overlaps a spinner, and Print which one was touched.

title: Enhanced Input & Building Pong
html:
Why Enhanced Input replaced the old system

Older Unreal projects bound raw keys in Project Settings. Modern UE5 uses Enhanced Input, a data-driven system that separates what the player wants to do from which key does it. This gives you runtime rebinding, layered control schemes (on foot vs in vehicle), modifiers (deadzones, swizzles), and triggers (hold, tap, double-tap) for free. Two asset types do the heavy lifting:

  • Input Action (IA) — an abstract intent like Move or Fire, with a value type: Boolean (button), Axis1D (single axis), or Axis2D (stick/2D move).
  • Input Mapping Context (IMC) — a prioritized set of bindings from physical keys to Input Actions, optionally with modifiers and triggers. You add contexts to a player at runtime.

Setting it up

Make sure the Enhanced Input plugin is enabled (it is by default in UE5) and that Project Settings → Input lists EnhancedInputComponent and EnhancedPlayerInput as the default classes. Then create the assets in the Content Browser: right-click → Input → Input Action for IA_PaddleMove (set Value Type = Axis1D), and Input → Input Mapping Context for IMC_Pong.

IMC_Pong (mapping context contents)
// IA_PaddleMove is Axis1D (returns -1..+1) Mapping: W --> IA_PaddleMove, Modifier: (none, +1) Mapping: S --> IA_PaddleMove, Modifier: Negate (-1) Mapping: Up Arrow --> IA_PaddleMove, Modifier: (none, +1) Mapping: Down Arrow --> IA_PaddleMove, Modifier: Negate (-1)

Adding the context and reading the action

A mapping context does nothing until you add it to the player's Enhanced Input Local Player Subsystem, usually on the pawn's BeginPlay. Then bind the Input Action in the Event Graph — Enhanced Input exposes a dedicated event node per action.

BP_Paddle · Event Graph (pseudo-graph)
// Variables: PaddleSpeed (Float)=900, MinY (Float)=-450, MaxY (Float)=450 Event BeginPlay --> GetController --> Cast PlayerController --> GetLocalPlayer --> Get EnhancedInputLocalPlayerSubsystem --> AddMappingContext(IMC_Pong, Priority = 0) EnhancedInputAction IA_PaddleMove (Triggered) ActionValue (Axis1D) --> axis delta = axis * PaddleSpeed * DeltaSeconds newY = Clamp(GetActorLocation.Y + delta, MinY, MaxY) SetActorLocation( Vector(X, newY, Z) )
Tip.
The Triggered event fires every frame the action is active, which is what you want for continuous movement. Use Started/Completed for one-shot button presses like jump or fire.

Building Pong

Pong is the perfect first complete game: two paddles, one ball, score. Build it on a flat plane viewed from a fixed top-down camera so it reads as a 2D field. Use simple cube meshes scaled into paddles and a ball, with BlockAll collision.

The ball

Give BP_Ball a ProjectileMovement component (set Initial Speed and Max Speed, Bounciness = 1, Friction = 0, and disable gravity). On BeginPlay, launch it with a random horizontal-ish velocity. ProjectileMovement reflects automatically off blocking surfaces, so the top/bottom walls and paddles bounce it for free.

BP_Ball · launch & scoring (pseudo-graph)
// Component: ProjectileMovement (Gravity=0, Bounciness=1, Friction=0) Event BeginPlay --> LaunchBall Function LaunchBall: dir.X = RandomBool ? 1 : -1 dir.Y = RandomFloatInRange(-0.5, 0.5) ProjectileMovement.Velocity = Normalize(dir) * 1200 Event ActorBeginOverlap(Other) --> Branch(Other == GoalLeft) True --> GameMode.AddScore(Right); ResetBall() False --> Branch(Other == GoalRight) True --> GameMode.AddScore(Left); ResetBall()

Scoring and reset

Put two thin overlap trigger boxes behind each paddle as goals. When the ball overlaps a goal, ask the GameMode to add a point to the other side, then re-center the ball and relaunch it. Store ScoreLeft and ScoreRight on BP_PongGameMode (or GameState) and print them on screen for now; we make a real UMG scoreboard in a later lesson.

Design law.
Keep one source of truth for state. Scores live in the GameMode/GameState, not duplicated on each paddle. Every other Actor reads or modifies that single copy.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade (Pong) — Pong

This starts a new genre — classic arcade (Pong). It is your first complete game, built on Enhanced Input.

  1. Enable Enhanced Input; create IA_PaddleMove (Axis1D) and IMC_Pong with W/S and Up/Down mappings (Negate on the down keys).
  2. Make BP_Paddle: add the mapping context on BeginPlay and move on IA_PaddleMove (Triggered), clamping Y between walls.
  3. Make BP_Ball with a ProjectileMovement (no gravity, full bounce) and a LaunchBall function with randomized direction.
  4. Build the field: floor plane, two side walls (BlockAll), two paddles, and two goal overlap boxes; add a fixed top-down camera.
  5. In BP_PongGameMode, store ScoreLeft/ScoreRight; on goal overlap add a point and call ResetBall.
  6. Add a simple AI for the right paddle (lerp its Y toward the ball's Y) or a second player mapping, then play to 11.

title: Collision, Hit Events & Breakout
html:
How Unreal collision works

Collision is governed by channels and responses. Every collision primitive has an Object Type (which channel it belongs to) and a table of responses to every channel: Ignore, Overlap, or Block. Two Actors only interact if both respond meaningfully to each other. The combination determines which event fires:

Response (both sides)ResultEvent fired
Block + BlockPhysical stop / bounceHit (OnComponentHit)
Overlap (at least one) + not BlockPass through, detectOverlap (Begin/End)
Ignore (either side)Nothingnone
Watch out.
For Hit events to fire, the moving component must have Simulation Generates Hit Events enabled. For Overlap events, the relevant components need Generate Overlap Events ticked. Forgetting these is the #1 reason collision callbacks silently never fire.

Collision presets and custom channels

Rather than hand-editing response tables on every Actor, use Collision Presets (BlockAll, OverlapAllDynamic, Pawn, etc.) in the component's Details. For game-specific logic, define your own channels in Project Settings → Collision — e.g. a Ball object channel and a Brick trace channel — so you can express rules precisely without fighting the engine's built-in physics channels.

Overlap vs Hit, in practice

Use Overlap for triggers and pickups where you want detection without physical reaction (Pong's goals, a coin, a damage volume). Use Hit when you want the physics/projectile system to actually bounce or stop the Actor and you also want to react in code (a ball striking a brick, a bullet impacting a wall).

BP_Brick · take a hit and destroy (pseudo-graph)
// Mesh collision: BlockAll, Simulation Generates Hit Events = true // Variables: Hitpoints (Int)=1, ScoreValue (Int)=100 Event OnComponentHit(OtherActor, HitComp, NormalImpulse, Hit) --> Cast OtherActor To BP_Ball Success --> Hitpoints = Hitpoints - 1 Branch(Hitpoints <= 0) True --> GameMode.AddScore(ScoreValue) --> SpawnEmitter(HitFX) --> DestroyActor(self)

Dynamic spawning and destroying

Two engine calls power most arcade games. SpawnActor (or the editor-friendly SpawnActorFromClass) creates an Actor at a transform at runtime; DestroyActor removes one. Breakout's brick wall is just a double loop that spawns bricks; the ball destroys them on contact. Snake's body grows by spawning a new segment Actor each time the head eats food.

BP_BreakoutGameMode · build the wall (pseudo-graph)
// Variables: Rows (Int)=5, Cols (Int)=10, Gap (Float)=120 Event BeginPlay --> ForLoop row 0..Rows-1 --> ForLoop col 0..Cols-1 pos = StartCorner + Vector(0, col*Gap, row*Gap*0.5) SpawnActorFromClass(BP_Brick, MakeTransform(pos))

Snake on a grid

Snake teaches discrete, grid-stepped movement instead of continuous physics. Drive it on a Timer (not Tick) so it steps every N seconds. Keep the body as an array of segment Actors; each step, move the head one cell in the current direction, and make every segment take the position of the one ahead of it. Eating food (an overlap) spawns a new tail segment and repositions the food. Self-overlap or wall-overlap ends the run.

BP_SnakeHead · grid step (pseudo-graph)
// Set Timer by Function Name 'StepSnake', Time=0.15, Looping=true Function StepSnake: // shift body: each segment follows the one in front ForEach(i from last down to 1) Body[i].Loc = Body[i-1].Loc head.Loc = head.Loc + (Direction * CellSize) Body[0].Loc = head.Loc Branch( head overlaps Food ) True --> GrowTail(); MoveFoodRandom() Branch( head overlaps self OR wall ) True --> GameOver()
Tip.
Buffer the next direction from input and only apply it at the start of StepSnake. This prevents the classic bug where a fast double-tap reverses the snake into itself within a single cell.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade (Breakout / Snake) — Breakout & Snake

This starts a new genre — classic arcade (Breakout and Snake). Breakout reuses the paddle and ball you built for Pong in lesson 3.4, then Snake is a fresh grid-based build.

  1. Reuse BP_Paddle and BP_Ball from Pong; reorient the camera so the field is a vertical playfield.
  2. Create BP_Brick with BlockAll + Simulation Generates Hit Events; on OnComponentHit from the ball, decrement Hitpoints and destroy at zero, adding score.
  3. In BP_BreakoutGameMode, spawn a grid of bricks on BeginPlay with a double ForLoop; lose a life when the ball overlaps the bottom goal.
  4. For Snake, define a Cell grid size; create BP_SnakeHead driven by a looping Timer, with a buffered direction from Enhanced Input.
  5. Maintain a body array; each step shift segments forward, grow on food overlap, and end the run on self/wall overlap.
  6. Track score in the GameMode for both games and print it on screen.

title: C++ Gameplay & the Reflection System
html:
Why drop into C++

Blueprints are excellent, but C++ gives you raw performance, access to the entire engine API, version-control-friendly text, and the ability to define base classes that designers then extend in Blueprint. The professional pattern is C++ for the system, Blueprint for the tuning: implement the gameplay class in code, expose knobs and functions to the editor, and let a Blueprint child set the mesh, numbers, and effects.

Adding C++ to a project

From the editor, Tools → New C++ Class. Choose a parent (Actor, Pawn, etc.), name it, and Unreal generates a header (.h) and source (.cpp) plus updates the build. You will need a compiler: Visual Studio (Windows) or Xcode (macOS) with the C++ toolchain. After editing code, click Compile in the editor (Live Coding) or build from the IDE.

The Unreal reflection system

Unreal layers a reflection system on top of C++ so the engine can see your classes, properties, and functions at runtime — enabling the editor, serialization, networking, garbage collection, and Blueprint exposure. You opt in with macros processed by the Unreal Header Tool (UHT):

  • UCLASS() — marks a class as reflected; pairs with the generated .generated.h include and a GENERATED_BODY() line.
  • UPROPERTY() — exposes a member variable to the editor, Blueprint, GC, and serialization.
  • UFUNCTION() — exposes a method so Blueprints (and the engine) can call or override it.
  • USTRUCT() / UENUM() — reflected structs and enums.

The specifiers in the parentheses control visibility. Common ones: EditAnywhere (tweak in Details), BlueprintReadWrite (get/set in graphs), BlueprintCallable (call a function from a graph), BlueprintImplementableEvent (defined in Blueprint, called from C++), and Category="..." to organize the panel.

Asteroid.h
#pragma once #include "CoreMinimal.h" #include "GameFramework/Actor.h" #include "Asteroid.generated.h" UCLASS() class MYGAME_API AAsteroid : public AActor { GENERATED_BODY() public: AAsteroid(); virtual void Tick(float DeltaSeconds) override; // Exposed to the editor and Blueprint child classes: UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere, BlueprintReadWrite, Category="Asteroid") float DriftSpeed = 200.f; UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere, BlueprintReadWrite, Category="Asteroid") int32 SizeTier = 3; // 3=large, 2=med, 1=small UPROPERTY(VisibleAnywhere) class UStaticMeshComponent* Mesh; UFUNCTION(BlueprintCallable, Category="Asteroid") void Split(); private: FVector Velocity; };
Asteroid.cpp
#include "Asteroid.h" #include "Components/StaticMeshComponent.h" AAsteroid::AAsteroid() { PrimaryActorTick.bCanEverTick = true; Mesh = CreateDefaultSubobject<UStaticMeshComponent>(TEXT("Mesh")); RootComponent = Mesh; } void AAsteroid::BeginPlay() { Super::BeginPlay(); const float ang = FMath::FRandRange(0.f, 360.f); Velocity = FRotator(0, ang, 0).Vector() * DriftSpeed; } void AAsteroid::Tick(float dt) { Super::Tick(dt); AddActorWorldOffset(Velocity * dt, true); WrapAroundScreen(); // torus wrap helper } void AAsteroid::Split() { if (SizeTier <= 1) { Destroy(); return; } for (int i = 0; i < 2; ++i) { AAsteroid* child = GetWorld()->SpawnActor<AAsteroid>( GetClass(), GetActorLocation(), FRotator::ZeroRotator); child->SizeTier = SizeTier - 1; child->SetActorScale3D(GetActorScale3D() * 0.6f); } Destroy(); }
Watch out.
Any UObject* pointer you want the garbage collector to keep alive must be a UPROPERTY(). A raw pointer with no UPROPERTY can be collected out from under you, causing crashes. This is the single most common C++-in-Unreal mistake.

Rebuilding Asteroids and Space Invaders in code

With the reflected base class above, an Asteroids ship spawns bullets (a small C++ AProjectile Actor) that, on hit, call Split() on the asteroid. Screen wrapping is a tiny C++ helper that mirrors position when an Actor leaves the bounds. For Space Invaders, write an AInvaderFormation Actor that holds a 2D array of invaders, steps them sideways on a timer, drops down and reverses at the edge, and periodically has a random column fire downward — all readable, fast C++ that designers tune via UPROPERTY values on a Blueprint child.

Tip.
Create a Blueprint child of your C++ class (right-click the class in the Content Browser) and assign the mesh, particle, and number tweaks there. Your C++ stays generic; the Blueprint becomes the designer-facing data.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade (Asteroids / Invaders) — Asteroids & Space Invaders in C++

This extends the classic-arcade genre from lessons 3.4-3.5 (Pong/Breakout/Snake), now rebuilding Asteroids and Space Invaders directly in C++ with reflection.

  1. Add a C++ AAsteroid (header/source as above) with UPROPERTY tuning and a Split() UFUNCTION.
  2. Add a C++ AProjectile and a APlayerShip Pawn; on projectile hit, call Split() on the asteroid.
  3. Implement a screen-wrap helper in C++ and reuse it on ship, bullets, and asteroids.
  4. Make Blueprint children of each C++ class to assign meshes and effects; tune speeds via the exposed properties.
  5. For Space Invaders, write AInvaderFormation that timer-steps a grid, edge-reverses with a downward drop, and fires from random columns.
  6. Score in a C++ AGameModeBase subclass; expose AddScore as BlueprintCallable so HUD work later can read it.

title: Paper2D, Sprites & a Side-Scrolling Platformer
html:
Unreal's 2D toolkit: Paper2D

Unreal is a 3D engine, but its Paper2D plugin gives first-class 2D: sprites, flipbook animations, tile sets, and tile maps, all rendered as flat geometry inside the same 3D world. That means a 2D platformer still benefits from Unreal's lighting, physics, and gameplay framework. Enable the plugin via Edit → Plugins → Paper2D if it is not already on, then restart.

From texture to sprite to flipbook

The 2D content pipeline has three steps:

  • Sprite — import a texture (set its Texture Group to 2D Pixels and filtering to Nearest for crisp pixel art), then right-click → Create Sprite. A sprite is a single image with a collision shape and pivot.
  • Sprite sheet extraction — for a packed sheet, use Apply Paper2D Texture Settings, then Extract Sprites (Grid or Auto) to slice it into many sprites at once.
  • Flipbook — right-click a set of sprites → Create Flipbook. A flipbook plays sprites as frames at a set FPS — your idle, run, and jump animations.
Tip.
For pixel art, set Filter = Nearest and disable mipmaps on the source texture, and pick a consistent Pixels Per Unit across all sprites so characters and tiles share a scale.

The PaperCharacter

Paper2D ships a ready-made PaperCharacter — a Character subclass whose mesh is a PaperFlipbookComponent instead of a skeletal mesh. It inherits the full CharacterMovementComponent, so you get gravity, walking, and jumping for free. Create BP_Hero from PaperCharacter, then constrain it to a 2D plane and wire animation.

Locking movement to a side-on plane

A side-scroller moves only along one horizontal axis plus vertical jump. In the CharacterMovement component, enable Constrain to Plane and set the plane normal to the depth axis (commonly X, so the character moves on the Y/Z plane). Also enable Snap to Plane at Start. Then point your camera straight down that axis.

BP_Hero · setup & movement (pseudo-graph)
// Components: SpringArm (rotated to look along +X), Camera (Orthographic) // CharacterMovement: Constrain to Plane = true, Plane Normal = (1,0,0) // Variables: IdleFB, RunFB, JumpFB (PaperFlipbook refs) Event BeginPlay --> AddMappingContext(IMC_Platformer) IA_Move (Triggered) Axis1D --> axis AddMovementInput( WorldDirection = (0,1,0), Scale = axis ) // flip the sprite to face travel direction: Branch(axis != 0) --> Sprite.SetWorldScale.Y = (axis > 0 ? 1 : -1) IA_Jump (Started) --> Jump // inherited from Character IA_Jump (Completed) --> StopJumping

Driving animation from state

Pick the right flipbook each frame based on the character's movement state. The Character exposes velocity and falling state, so a small Tick (or, better, a dedicated function) chooses idle / run / jump.

BP_Hero · UpdateAnimation (pseudo-graph)
Function UpdateAnimation (call from Event Tick): falling = GetCharacterMovement.IsFalling() speed = GetVelocity().Length() Branch(falling) True --> Sprite.SetFlipbook(JumpFB) False --> Branch(speed > 10) True --> Sprite.SetFlipbook(RunFB) False --> Sprite.SetFlipbook(IdleFB)

Tuning the jump for good feel

Platformer feel lives in the CharacterMovement numbers. Raise Jump Z Velocity for a higher jump, lower Gravity Scale (or set it >1 for snappier falls), and increase Air Control so players can steer mid-jump. Max Walk Speed, Ground Friction, and Braking Deceleration control how crisp the run feels.

Design law.
Great jumps are forgiving. Add coyote time (allow a jump for a few frames after leaving a ledge) and jump buffering (queue a jump pressed just before landing). Both are small timer tricks that make controls feel fair.

Building the level

Lay out platforms with sprites or a Tile Map (a Paper2D asset painted from a Tile Set). Give ground tiles a collision profile that blocks the Pawn. Add a side-view orthographic camera on a spring arm so the world reads truly flat, and have the camera follow the hero along the movement axis only.

🎮 Build This: Platformer — Side-Scrolling Platformer

This starts a new genre — the 2D side-scrolling platformer, built with Paper2D and reusing the Enhanced Input skills from lesson 3.4.

  1. Enable Paper2D; import a character sheet, extract sprites, and make IdleFB, RunFB, JumpFB flipbooks.
  2. Create BP_Hero from PaperCharacter; constrain CharacterMovement to a plane and snap to it at start.
  3. Add IMC_Platformer with IA_Move (Axis1D) and IA_Jump; wire move, sprite flipping, and the inherited Jump/StopJumping.
  4. Add UpdateAnimation to swap flipbooks by velocity and falling state.
  5. Build a level from a Paper2D Tile Map (or platform sprites) with blocking collision; add an orthographic side-view camera on a spring arm that follows the hero.
  6. Tune Jump Z Velocity, Gravity Scale, and Air Control; add coyote time and jump buffering for feel.

title: World Design, Gates & a Metroidvania
html:
What makes a metroidvania

A metroidvania is an interconnected world the player explores non-linearly, where progress is gated by abilities rather than keys to specific doors. You see a high ledge you cannot reach, find the double-jump elsewhere, and return. The genre rests on three pillars: a connected map, ability gates, and persistent player progression. We build it directly on the platformer from lesson 3.7.

Designing interconnected space

Sketch your world as a graph of rooms connected by edges (doorways, corridors). Each edge can be open or gated. Good metroidvania design plants the sight of a gate early so players remember it: a chasm too wide for a normal jump, a wall that needs a dash, a high alcove needing double-jump. Keep a real map (even on paper) so you can guarantee the world loops back on itself and every ability unlocks at least one previously-blocked path.

Level streaming for a seamless world

Rather than one giant level (slow to load, heavy on memory) or hard loading screens between rooms, use Level Streaming so areas load and unload around the player. In modern UE5 the cleanest approach is World Partition with streaming, but for a hand-authored metroidvania, classic Streaming Levels mapped to trigger volumes give you precise control.

  • Author each region as its own sub-level in the persistent level (Window → Levels).
  • Place a trigger volume at each region's entrance.
  • On overlap, call Load Stream Level for the room ahead and Unload Stream Level for rooms far behind.
BP_RegionStreamer · stream rooms (pseudo-graph)
// Box trigger at a region border; RoomToLoad / RoomToUnload are Name vars Event OnComponentBeginOverlap(Other) --> Cast Other To BP_Hero Success --> LoadStreamLevel(RoomToLoad, MakeVisible=true, Block=false) --> UnloadStreamLevel(RoomToUnload, Block=false)
Tip.
Stream with Block On Load = false so the game does not hitch, and preload the next room a little before the player reaches the border so it is ready by the time they cross.

Persistent player abilities

Abilities must survive moving between rooms and be readable by gates anywhere. Store them as flags on a long-lived object — the PlayerState or a GameInstance (which persists across level loads). A clean approach is a single bitmask or a set of booleans plus an enum of ability ids.

BP_GameInstance · ability flags (pseudo-graph)
// EAbility enum: DoubleJump, Dash, WallCling, ... // Variable: UnlockedAbilities (Set of EAbility) Function GrantAbility(Ability) --> UnlockedAbilities.Add(Ability) Function HasAbility(Ability) -> bool : return UnlockedAbilities.Contains(Ability)

Collectible upgrades that grant abilities

An upgrade pickup is just an overlap that grants an ability and removes itself. Because the flag lives on the GameInstance, the new power persists into every other room immediately.

BP_AbilityPickup · grant on touch (pseudo-graph)
// Variable: GrantedAbility (EAbility), set per placed pickup Event ActorBeginOverlap(Other) --> Cast Other To BP_Hero Success --> GetGameInstance.GrantAbility(GrantedAbility) --> PlayFX(); DestroyActor(self)

Gating with abilities

Two kinds of gate. Hard gates are physical barriers a power removes (a wall that only a dash crosses, a force-field that drops once you have an upgrade). Soft gates are traversal challenges that are simply impossible without the ability — a gap only the double-jump clears. Implement the double-jump by extending the hero from lesson 3.7: read HasAbility(DoubleJump) and raise the Character's Jump Max Count.

BP_Hero · ability-aware jump (pseudo-graph)
Event BeginPlay --> RefreshAbilities Function RefreshAbilities: JumpMaxCount = GameInstance.HasAbility(DoubleJump) ? 2 : 1 bCanDash = GameInstance.HasAbility(Dash) IA_Dash (Started) --> Branch(bCanDash) True --> LaunchCharacter( FacingDir * DashImpulse, XYOverride=true )
Watch out.
Re-run RefreshAbilities whenever an ability is granted and whenever the hero respawns in a newly streamed room. If you only read flags once at level start, a power picked up mid-room will not take effect until the next load.
Design law.
Always show the lock before the key. Let players glimpse a gated area so the later unlock delivers an “I can go there now” payoff — that recognition loop is the heart of the genre.
🎮 Build This: Metroidvania — Ability-Gated World

This extends the platformer from lesson 3.7 into a full metroidvania, adding interconnected streamed rooms, collectible upgrades, and ability gates.

  1. Map your world as a room graph; mark which edges are gated by DoubleJump, Dash, and WallCling.
  2. Author each region as a sub-level; add BP_RegionStreamer trigger volumes that load/unload rooms around the hero.
  3. Create BP_GameInstance with an UnlockedAbilities set plus GrantAbility/HasAbility.
  4. Make BP_AbilityPickup that grants an ability on overlap and destroys itself; place one per power.
  5. Extend BP_Hero with RefreshAbilities (double-jump via JumpMaxCount, dash via LaunchCharacter); call it on BeginPlay and after each grant.
  6. Place hard gates (dash-only walls, drop-on-upgrade force-fields) and soft gates (double-jump gaps) so each ability opens a previously-seen path.
